title: Assignment 1
text:
My first assignment has four parts.


I watched the video titled ãVeri Bilimi ve Endû¥stri Mû¥hendisliái ûzerine Sohbetler - Kerem Demirtaé & Erdi Dasdemirã.
In this video, the connection between data science and industrial engineering is explored. The conversation centers around how industrial engineers can take advantage of data-driven decision-making, statistical thinking, optimization, and analytical approaches. It also highlights that data science is growing in importance within business settings, as companies need to make quicker and more precise decisions based on data.
The core message of the video is that industrial engineering and data science are two deeply connected fields. Industrial engineers can leverage data science tools to analyze complex systems, improve processes, support decision-making, and develop more efficient solutions.





Question:
Why is data science important for industrial engineers?
Answer: Data science matters for industrial engineers because it allows them to analyze complex systems, recognize patterns in data, and arrive at better decisions. Industrial engineers regularly deal with production planning, optimization, process improvement, quality control, and decision-making challenges. Data science strengthens these areas by offering tools for data analysis, prediction, visualization, and evidence-based problem solving.



Question:
Which of the following best explains the relationship between industrial engineering and data science?
A. Industrial engineering and data science are entirely separate fields.
B. Data science is solely concerned with software development and has no relevance to industrial engineering.
C. Both fields rely on analytical thinking and data to improve systems and guide decision-making.
D. Industrial engineers should stay away from using data when tackling real-world problems.
Correct Answer: C. Both fields rely on analytical thinking and data to improve systems and guide decision-making.




In this section, I use the built-in mtcars dataset in R. The dataset contains measurements for 32 cars and 11 variables.

data(mtcars)

head(mtcars)

                   mpg cyl disp  hp drat    wt  qsec vs am gear carb
Mazda RX4         21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.620 16.46  0  1    4    4
Mazda RX4 Wag     21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.875 17.02  0  1    4    4
Datsun 710        22.8   4  108  93 3.85 2.320 18.61  1  1    4    1
Hornet 4 Drive    21.4   6  258 110 3.08 3.215 19.44  1  0    3    1
Hornet Sportabout 18.7   8  360 175 3.15 3.440 17.02  0  0    3    2
Valiant           18.1   6  225 105 2.76 3.460 20.22  1  0    3    1




The following function, named compute_stats, takes a numeric vector as input and returns a named list containing the mean, median, variance, interquartile range, minimum, and maximum.

compute_stats <- function(x) {
  list(
    mean = mean(x),
    median = median(x),
    variance = var(x),
    IQR = IQR(x),
    minimum = min(x),
    maximum = max(x)
  )
}




In this part, I use a for loop to iterate over all numerical columns of the mtcars dataset. For each column, I apply the compute_stats function and print the column name together with the calculated statistics.

for (col_name in names(mtcars)) {
  cat("\nColumn:", col_name, "\n")
  print(compute_stats(mtcars[[col_name]]))
}

For the apply function, I first convert the mtcars data frame into a matrix. Then I use margin 2 because the function should be applied column-wise.

mtcars_matrix <- as.matrix(mtcars)

apply(mtcars_matrix, 2, compute_stats)

In this section, I use the polls_us_election_2016 dataset from the dslabs package.

library(dslabs)

data("polls_us_election_2016")
polls_data <- polls_us_election_2016




my_first <- "Yesim"
my_birth_year <- 1992

k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
k

new_data <- polls_data

for (col_name in names(new_data)) {
  
  if (is.numeric(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- my_birth_year + k
  }
  
  if (is.character(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
  
  if (is.factor(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]] <- as.character(new_data[[col_name]])
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
}

title: Assignment 1
text:
I watched the video titled ãVeri Bilimi ve Endû¥stri Mû¥hendisliái ûzerine Sohbetler - Kerem Demirtaé & Erdi Dasdemirã.
In this video, the relationship between data science and industrial engineering is discussed. The conversation focuses on how industrial engineers can benefit from data-driven decision-making, statistical thinking, optimization, and analytical methods. It also emphasizes that data science is becoming increasingly important in business environments because organizations need to make faster and more accurate decisions based on data.
The main message of the video is that industrial engineering and data science are closely related fields. Industrial engineers can use data science tools to analyze complex systems, improve processes, support decision-making, and create more efficient solutions.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Question:
Why is data science important for industrial engineers?
Answer:
Data science is important for industrial engineers because it helps them analyze complex systems, understand patterns in data, and make better decisions. Industrial engineers often work on production planning, optimization, process improvement, quality control, and decision-making problems. Data science supports these areas by providing tools for data analysis, prediction, visualization, and evidence-based decision-making.



Question:
Which of the following best explains the relationship between industrial engineering and data science?
A. Industrial engineering and data science are completely unrelated fields.
B. Data science only focuses on software development and has no connection with industrial engineering.
C. Both fields use analytical thinking and data to improve systems and support decision-making.
D. Industrial engineers should avoid using data when solving real-life problems.
Correct Answer: C. Both fields use analytical thinking and data to improve systems and support decision-making.

For the apply function, I first convert the mtcars data frame into a matrix. Then I use margin 2 because the function should be applied column-wise.

mtcars_matrix <- as.matrix(mtcars)

apply(mtcars_matrix, 2, compute_stats)

title: Assignment 1
text:
In this section, I use the polls_us_election_2016 dataset from the dslabs package.

library(dslabs)

data("polls_us_election_2016")
polls_data <- polls_us_election_2016




my_first <- "Yesim"
my_birth_year <- 1992

k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
k

[1] 10






if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(polls_data, k)
} else {
  tail(polls_data, k)
}

total_na <- sum(is.na(polls_data))
total_na

[1] 11604



na_per_column <- sort(colSums(is.na(polls_data)), decreasing = TRUE)
head(na_per_column, 8)

rawpoll_mcmullin adjpoll_mcmullin  rawpoll_johnson  adjpoll_johnson 
            4178             4178             1409             1409 
           grade       samplesize            state        startdate 
             429                1                0                0 






new_data <- polls_data

for (col_name in names(new_data)) {
  
  if (is.numeric(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- my_birth_year + k
  }
  
  if (is.character(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
  
  if (is.factor(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]] <- as.character(new_data[[col_name]])
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
}





if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(new_data, k)
} else {
  tail(new_data, k)
}
